: CURRENT AFFAIRS.

We paid a brief visit to the ‘‘ International ”’ Exhi-
bition on Saturday last in the leafy month of June. A
system of hot-water pipes for the benefit of the forlorn
individuals seated in the open round the Tommy Atkin’s
band would have its advantages. We were only a short
time at the Exhibition, and certainly it is a big thing
in the way of mancuyring for a knighthood for Mr.
William Murphy of the ‘‘ Nationalist Organ.” Mr.
Murphy, of the ‘‘ Independent,’” truly went a long way
‘Tound for his knighthood; and the carts of his

. ““Nationalist Organ ”’ formed quite a prominent feature
in Sunday’s great Language Procession. Mr. Murphy’s
“ Nationalist Organ ”’ wants to have it both wafs. It
forced the ‘“ International ’’ Exhibition on a reluctant

_ country, and yet it sends its carts into the Language
Procession, in which one of the tableaux represented an
Trish labourer with his wife and children cowering at
his feet, and a sign post is inscribed “ To the Emigrant
Ship,” and ‘“To Herbert Park.” We cannot say

 whether the ‘“ Independent’’ carts paraded before this
tableau or after it; but we might add that some of the
firms that took advantage of the great language procession
as an opportunity for advertising their goods are also
~ showing at the ““International ™ Exhibition! Indeed,
if the Gaclic League were to boycott the firms that are

showing at Terbert Park, it would have almost to
advise the boycotting of Irish industries!

We only have a general impression of the affair, as we
took no notes at our first visit. It is a great site cer-
tainly for the Canadian advertisement for emigrants.
Canada is certainly under a debt of gratitude to Mr.
Murphy’s ““ Nationalist Organ.”” The Canadian pavilion
is an elaborate business, and contains extracts from the
writings of Mr. T. P. O’Connor, ‘‘the leader of the .
Irish race’” in England. Canada is not a green
country’; we being a green people, provided a splendid
site for Canada’s advertisement for fully grown settlers,
and Canada, being a shrewd country, has taken full
advantage of it. As a full-grown man dumped on the
shores of Canada of the snows is worth about £250, it is
sound finance, from Canada’s point of view, to go to
the expense of setting up the Canadian pavilion; for
100 emigrants are worth £25,000 to it. We being a
green people, offer Canada a site for its advertisement,
and sure, if only a thousand Ivishmen emigrate as a
consequence, Canada gains £250,000, and if Mr. Wil-
liam Murphy, of the ‘‘ Nationalist Organ,” is made a
Knight, ought not Ireland rejoice at the bargain!

Ireland did not want an ‘‘ International ’’ Exhibi-
tion at this stage of its development; but Mr. William
Murphy, of the ‘‘ Independent,”” no doubt ‘‘ animated
solely by a patriotic resolve,”” wanted it, and it was
forced on the country and on the manufacturers. It is
there now, and several of the free and independent
delegates to the recent convention went out there—
whether they cheered several times or not when they
arrived we cannot say. People are flocking in dribs and
drabs to it from the provinces; the restaurants aud
several other business places in Dublin are complaining,
and the Tivoli music hall, we understand, is hard hit;
so also, no doubt, is the ‘‘ Star Theatre of Varieties,”’
even though it is a guarantor!

But the chief fact is that the Garrison, aided by Mr.
William Murphy, of the ‘‘ Nationalist Organ,”’ carried
their project, whilst the project of a National Exhibi-
tion, with which Mr. Martyn, Mr. Sweetman and
Alderman Cole were prominently associated, did not
blossom,.not: to say come to fruition. The ‘‘ Interna-
tional ”’ went on, and we find as a matter of fact that
the TIrish manufacturers have largely gone into
it.  When Murphy’s ‘“International” was being
pulled through, we take it that in their
business judgment they decided that they had
better go in, and in they went, and judging from our
brief visit they make a brave shew in the battle
ground forced upon them by the busybodies who ran
the affair against the express wishes of the country.
Enterprising manufacturers like Messrs. Varian, Good-
bodys, Williams and Woods, Barringtons, Pattersons,
Tovenden and Orr, Aylesburys, Hopkins and Hopkins,
Hicks, Bullock and Co., Dixon and Co., Gallaghers,
cte., etc., have gone in, we take it, in self-defence. °

The Exhibition oxcites no enthusiasm on any side.
Bray and Kingstown, and such summer day resorts, will
probably be very hardly hit by it as well as the city
restaurants.  However, that is their look-out.  The
water-chute, the switchback, etc., will not leave much
spare cash in the pockets of the ‘ hell of a fellow ’’ who
frequents the Exhibition. But those serious and sub-
stantial people who do go may as well take a good look
at those Irish industries as are represented. It is in-
teresting to mote that glove-making has been established

in Tipperary, and this is an industry of practically un-

limited possibilities. A well-known British firm of

THE 'LEADER.

glove-makers have started this Tipperary industry, and

- there is a working exhibition well worthy of inspection.
We wonder will Ireland have to depend on British initia-
tive for a great part of her real industrial revival,
whilst our Mr. John Sweetmans do the tall talk about
boycotting England? The fate of one industry due to
British initiative—Kynoch’s of Arklow—is at the
moment an Irish national question! Next week we
intend to pay another visit to the ‘“ International,”” and
will-have more to say about it.

Feill a’ Chomuinn Ghaedhealach is a Highland
Bazaar being organised for the purpose of raising funds
to carry out the objects of the Chomuinn Ghaedhealach,
which are the teaching of Gaelic, the development of
Gaelic literature, music and art, and the encourage-
ment of home industries amongst Scotch Gaels.  The
objects, it will be seen, are similar to those of the
Gaelic League in Ireland; but Scotland has not an
anti-national Ascendancy party as we are cursed with
in this country. This Scotch Gaelic Bazaar is therefore
quite fashionable. We have before us a printed list
of those who have promised to support the Bazaar.
They include two duchesses, one dowager duchess, four
marchionesses, six countesses, one dowager countess,
two viscountesses, thirty-two women of the rank of lady,
two dukes, three marquises, five earls, “one viscount,
four lords, fifteen, baronets. These classes of people in
TIreland are all, with few exceptions, divorced from the
country—they are more, they are decidedly anti-
national.

They have something to fight for in Arklow, and so
at the demonstration on Saturday Nationalist and
Unionist stood together. The more Ireland has to fight
for the harder she will fight; it is because we are so poor
that there is so little real national fight in us. Arklow
has tasted a little prosperity, and she is in no mind to
be robbed of it without knowing the reason why. We
ought to be able to make as united a fight on the over-
taxation question as on the Arklow industry. Such
fights keep our faces to a common foe, anyway. In
the last analysis the reason why there are Unionists in
this country is that Unionists are the party. of Ascend-
ancy, and they consider that in a shuflling of the cards
they would not necessarily have so many trumps; that
the Nationalists would stand a fairer chance of getting
trumps. Where there is no question of a shuffle of the
TIrish cards, but of saving an industry like that at Ark-
low, or tackling the over-taxation question, there is no
reason why Unionists and Nationalists should not fight
shoulder to shoulder, and, as a matter of fact, they have
done so in both instances.  Unionists think it pays

_them at the expense of the Nationalists to be Unionists;
if a syndicate of Yankee multi-millionaires came over
and paid them to be Nationalists, the Unionist party
would quickly evaporate. In cases where the Unionists
fear that they would lose by being put into open compe-
tition with Nationalists, they will be Ascendancy men
_ first and Irishmen afterwards; and the prospect of get-
ting their share out of a prosperous, go-ahead country
is insufficlent to induce them to relax their grip on their
unfair share of what a stick-in-the-mud country has to
offer. Tt is well to recognise the condition under which
Nationalists and Unionists will unite and will not unite.
It is absurd to be crying ‘‘all classes’’ in a situation
where one of the classes thinks its interests are antago-
nist to unity. All classes can unite on the over-taxation
question as they united on Saturday at Arklow.

Mr. T. A. Cocking, a director of Kynoch’s, Ltd., is
certainly a vigorous speaker. Referring to the state-
ment made in Parliament that there had heen an explo-

JuNE 15, n967.

sion with Arklow gelignite in a Welsh mine, Mr. Cock-
ing said that the statement put into tlie mouth of the
Home Secretary was & deliber_a.be lie. This coming from
a responsible person-like a director of Kynoch’s, Ltd.,
is a startling indictment against the Home Office.

Mr. Cocking paid some handsome tributes o the Ark-
low workers. e said:—

The Arklow oxplosives were recognised for their merit in every
patt of tho world (applause). It was not necessary to soll Arklow
oxplosives under tho prico of compotitors. Tho Arklow oxplosives
Would generally command_a higher prico solely by thoir sheor
morit, and the present position must justify anyone in attributing
{he sucoss of the factory to the perseverance, the stoadiness, and
tho intolligenco of the people in making the explosive (applause).
Arklow fonred no trade rivalry. Arklow could hold its own any-
whoro and go on at this ratc of progress. And whatever might
happen in the future, the past alono had pointed out ono thing,
and that was that if they wanted to have a factory where the
omploycos would take an intolligent intorest in their Worl, would
work loyally with their employers, would, by their own in-
dustry and perseverance, make a succ of what they took up,
then that factory would be in Arklow, or some other place in
Troland (applause).  Arklow feared no trade rival. ~ But there
wero othor things which it did fear. It must have the same treat-
ment as its competitors in Great Britain (hear, hear), and that was
the only thing which Arklow wanted. It wanled no favours; it
wanted simple justice in the same way as its English competitors.

Mr. Cocking gave a startling instance of the Home
Office’s prejudice against Ireland in a matter where,
however, the Home Office were beaten by Messrs.
Kynoch. The Home Office attempted to refuse to allow
Messrs. Kynoch to manufacture a certain powder in
Arklow which they gave them full liberty to manufac-
ture in Essex. The Home Office said they hadn’t con-
fidence in the people of Arklow to justify them,.and
that they didn’t propose to license any new explosives
at Arklow, but that they could make as much as they
liked at Essex. This reveals a very startling prejudice
to the detriment of Irish industry, and if the Home
Office had not a great, and we might say explosive, firm
like Kynochs to deal with, the Home Office would prob-
ably have succeeded in the attempted boycott.

Amongst the other speakers was Mr. Marcus Geed-
body, of Dublin. FHe said :—‘ All Irish manufacturers
wanted was a fair field and no favour. Governments
might go in and out, but the permanent official was
always there, and the leopard could not change his spots.
Ie personally, in his own business, knew what hap-
pened. He was very pleased to hear what Mr. Cocking
said about labour in Arklow. His (Mr. Goodbody’s)
firm had started one industry in Ireland, and another
branch of the family had started another industry; and
they had found that Irish labour was able to compete
with any labour in the world.”’” The Trish Parliamen-
tary Party may have little or no power now in the way
of Iome Rule, but this Arklow business is one of the
many things that crop up from time to time in which
they can render service to the country, and help to
justify their existence on ‘‘the floor.””

Mr. Murphy’s ‘‘ Nationalist organ,”’ in its leading
article of Monday last discussing the Arklow affair,
said—“ Want of confidence in the Irish people is at the
bottom of the Arklow boycott.”” Perhaps as we are on
the question of want of confidence in Irish people, the
““ Independent ”’ would explain the want of confidence
in Trish people that resulted in the handing over of the
catering at the ‘‘ International >’ to a London firm? And
as it is about it, it might clear up all the other little
want of confidences that the Exhibition suggests! Tf
Mr. Shanks and the Finance Committee of the ¢ Tnter- -
national ”’ were the English Home Office how furiously
the ‘“ Independent’’ would, in its fresh capacity as a
“ Nationalist organ,”” wave green flags.

You can see them but you don’t hear them.

The London ‘‘Globe,”” one of the organs of the
“Carrion Crows,’”’ states that ‘' the King and Queen
are to lay the foundation-stone of the University Col-
lege of North Wales on the 9th of that month (July),
and next morning they will slip across from Holyhead
to Kingstown, and spend a couple of nights at the Vice-
regal Lodge.”” The shrewd reader will kindly observe
that his Majesty is not to lay any foundation-stona of
a University College in Dublin. e is to go to the
Exhibition, and to the Leopardstown races, and
mayhap he will honour Ireland by making a knight of
Mr. William Murphy, of the ‘‘ Nationalist organ.’”

The ‘“ Carrion Crows’’ take a great interest in ‘ the
South and West.”” Well, perhaps one of them would
ask one of the British ministers—Mr. Haldane, we
suppose, would be the man—if the authorities of the
British army are in order in going out of their way to
facilitate a bigot who has a vacancy for which no
Papist, not even a Papist army pensioner, need apply.
If Captain Craig would not care to ask this question
perhaps Mr. M‘Veagh or Mr. Delany would do so.

The following letter was addressed to the Command-
ing Royal Engineer, Dublin Sub-district, Dublin
Castle :—

‘¢ Estate Office, Lismore Castle, Lismore,
“99th April, 1907.

‘“Dear Sir—I am looking for a Protestant R. E.
Pensioner to fill the position of Timekeeper and Over-
seer here on the Duke of Devonshire’s Estate. -THe
must be a trustworthy, active man with a knowledge of
building. His pay would be 16s. per week, small house,
rent free, gratuity of £5 per year, 30s. per year for
potatoes, and a suit of clothes every second year.

I shall feel very much obliged if you can recommend
me a man of that class.—Yours faithfully, "

‘“L. M. FirzGERALD.”’

What did the Commanding Royal Engineer, Dublin
Castle, do with this application? Did he write to Mr.
L. M. Fitzgerald rebuking him for his ‘‘ no Papist need
apply ”’ letter, and did he communicate with the Duke of
Devonshire protesting against receiving such an insult-
ing and bigoted application from his Lismore estate?
Nothing of the kind. He apparently tried Dublin, but
could find no Protestant suitable for the job!  This
exclusive dealing and boycotting letter was circulated
through Dublin by Dublin Castle, but when Colonel
D. F. MacCarthy, C.R.E., Dublin Sub-district, failed
to find a suitable man for this boycotting and exclusive
dealing application he did not rest satisfied. It was

We hope that a few questions on this matter will be
asked by some of the Irish members of Parliament. We
scarcely venture to hope that there is any Papist peer in
the House of Lords who would have the pluck to bring
the matter before that House of which the Duke cf
Devonshire is such a heavy, if not exalted, member.
But perhaps Lord Dunraven would give proof >f his
tolerance to all, irrespective of religious profession, by
calling attention to this infamous piece of bigotry and
boycotting emanating from the Duke of Devonshire’s
estate offices, and fostered and fondled in Dublin Castle
by an official, we presume a number of officials, who are
paid out of the taxes. It would be tco much to sug-
gest that Lord Ashtown would put this example of boy-
cotting from ‘‘ the South’’ in the *“ Carrion Crow " of
which he is compiler.

Several matters concerning the Post Office in Ire-
land have been brought before our readers from time
to time. It is bad enough that human dumps from
Britain are dumped over the heads of Irish officials into
good positions. But there is also the important ques-
tion of the Post Office requirement in the way of goods.
One interested in this matter has been good enough to
compile a schedule from the Parliamentary Estimates.
The compilation does not profess to be absolutely accu-
rate, as it is impossible to tell in many cases from the
Estimates what the actual Irish expenditure is. How-
ever, in the opinion of a correspondent of ours who has
seen this schedule, it may be stated without contradic-
tion that on an expenditure of £2,077,000 under cer-
tain sub-heads not more than £40,000, probably less,
is expended in Ireland. No wonder emigration coun-
tinues. Our correspondent suggests that perhaps Mr.
Tield will press his inquiries in this direction, and call
for a Committee of Inquiry into the subject. But does
any good come out of these Committees of Inquiry?

Father C. Fitzgerald presided at the recent meeting
of the (:}eneru.l Committee of the Munster Feis, and there
were nine other priests also present. Amongst others
at the meeting were—Professor Stockley, Mr. W.
McDonald, J.P., Chairman of the Cork County Council ;
M}‘. M. Murphy, solicitor; Mr. J. F. Ilogan, manager,
Hibernian Bank.

If the Munster Feis is to be the really great success
that its promoters are working vigorously to make it
the subscriptions must flow in, and, judging by the first
list of subscriptions, it is very likely that the friends of
the:language in Munster will see to it that the funds
are adequate. Five gentlomen, namely, Canon
Qohalan, P.P., V.F., Bantry, Professor Windle, Ire-
sident Queen’s College, Cork; Mr. Augustine Roche,
M.P.; Alderman Simcox, and Mr. Barry Egan, have
offered special prizes. We must say the first list looks
rather like a ‘ priest-ridden’’ collection; the laity

In these days when there is so much anxiety expressed
in some quarters about the laity and the management
of Primary Schools it is interesting to note that, when
it comes to subscribing to the funds of a great educa-
tional function like the Munster Feis, the laity take a
rather back seat. Are the clergy crushing them out!
Aro the laity ‘forbidden to subscribe! Indeed, the list
of first. subscriptions to the Munster Feis is rather
humiliating reading for laymen—do they want the
clergy to supply nearly all the money? We trust that
our lay readers all over the world, who were born in
Munster, will not leave it to be said they allowed the
clergy to do a most disproportionate share of the sub-
scribing. The first list is a very strong one, and in-
dicates the vigour with which the honorary officers of
the Feis are working; in the next list, although we are
not anti-clericals, we would like to see the laity sub-
scribing about five times as much as the priests!

. The Meath Feis will be held at Kells on June 29th
and 30th. There will be an Industrial Exhibition in
connection with the Feis.

A certain Irish manufacturer thought that the “ In-
ternational *’ Exhibition would be inundated with Eng-
lish trade rivals, and he consequently laid himself out
in self-defence to meet the anticipated competition as a
business man, and spent a lot of money on his stall.
Other manufacturers, he says, did likewise for a like
reason. And now they find that the great competition
from England was only a sort of nightmare; it has not
materialised. Irish manufacturers are, in many respects,
predominant at this so-called ‘‘ International.”” This
Irish manufacturer did not go into the Exhibition for
political reasons or for love of it; he went in as a wide-
awake manufacturer, ready to fight his cornmer in any
contest that might be forced upon him. Now, he asks,
are he and his fellow-Irish manufacturers to be boy-
cotted, for he puts it that boycotting the Exhibition is
boycotting the Irish manufacturers who are showing
there? It is easy enough to look at the question from
this Irish manufacturer’s point of view, and one has to
admit the force of his contention. The indignation
of those who were against the dumping of this project
in Ireland against Ireland’s express wishes should be
largely directed against the so-called ‘‘ Nationalist
organ "’—the ‘‘ Independent.”” It was that paper that
practically forced it on a reluctant country. = It would
appear that it is not prospering overmuch for all its
flying in the face of Irish opinion, for we see it is try-
ing to stimulate a bogus circulation by some gambling
scheme—offering a chance of a few shillings to those who
buy it! What a pretty ‘‘ Nationalist organ,”’ to be
sure! Why does it not make itself worth a ha’penny
to the Irish daily newspaper readers, and sell itself on
its merits without further stimulating the gambling
instinct, or rather gambling acquired taste, of our
demoralised people?

tainty of March weather during the earlier part of last
Sunday.  Fortunately as the hour for the Language

in June, however, has advantages over an uncertain
day in March, and there can be no doubt that those
who took part in the procession, and those who lined
the route, lost nothing in comfort by the change of
date. The procession was, as usual, big and imposing,
as an indication of the interest aroused in Dublin by the
Irish-Ircland propaganda. We hope that the result of
the collection will prove that the interest is earnest

Mr. Boyle’s comedy, ‘“ The Eloquent Dempsey,”” has
just been published by the well-known publishing firm,
O’Donoghue and Co., Dublin. The production of the
play in book-form will be welcomed by many who have
not had an opportunity of seeing it on the stage, as well
as by many who, having seen it acted, are anxious to
renew ‘‘ The Eloquent Dempsey’s ’’ acquaintance. We
understand that parts of the author’s MS. were omitted
from the comedy as already acted. The new publica-
tion repairs this defect, and fully represents the author’s
original design. We are sure that ‘“The Eloquent
Dempsey,’” the price of which is 1s. net, will have a
large circle of readers.

Messrs. John Shuley and Co., Stafford St., Dublin,
have added an important branch of industry to their
extensive business. Pens are a general necessity, and
must represent a big item of Irish expenditure.
Hitherto all the pens used here had to be imported,
but we are now glad to announce the introduction of
the pen manufacture to Ireland. Messrs. Shuley and
Co. have started with the making of steel pens cousist-
ing of two kinds, broad and fine pointed, but intend as
soon as possible to include the other classes of the pen
industry. The manufacture involves a variety of
machined processes by which the finished article is
evolved from the original plain sheet steel. The name
of the new Irish pen is the ““J. S.”” pen, and we have
no doubt that-thousands of Irish writers will now not
only hold their notepaper up to the light to see that it
is Irish, but will also be anxious to secure an Irish pen
to write on it.

A reader of the LEADER, who resides in Cape Colony,
oxpects to be in Dublin next month. He says in his
lotter :—“ T am almost certain I could push the sale of
lots of Irish goods in this market.” Te encloses us a
small advertisement for insertion in what he styles ‘ the
good old Leaver.”” The advertisement will be found in
another column. Ie is open to negotiate with Irish
manufacturers as a representative on commission in the
Cape Colony market ; and Irish manufacturers who wish
to push their trade in that part of the world, would
do well to put themselves in communication with him.

The Knockvilla Feis---or perhaps it ought to be called
Tather Matt Ryan’s Feis—comes off next Sunday. The
date was fixed about a year back, and we are sorry to
hear that the success of this Feis is being prejudiced by
the holding of a G.A.A. fixture at Thurles on the same
day. The Feis had been duly sanctioned by the Execu-
tive of the Gaelic League, and had been announced in
newspapers, and on posters, in the neighbouring
parishes. What is the object of the G.A.A. in arrang-
ing a football match for the same date? We hope that
the G.A.A.—for its own sake as well as for the sake of
the Knockvilla Feis—will postpone the match at Thurles.

Wherever Father Matt Maguire, late of ‘‘ Irish Dro-
more,”’ goes, there the Trish Language Movement is
planted vigorously. He is now P.P. ot Kilskeery, and
on the 15th August the Kilskeery Feis will be held at
Trillick, Co. Tyrone. Twelve months ago not even a
branch of the Gaelic League existed in the parish, yet
in a couple of months a Feis with eighty-six competi-
tions will be held. DPrizes are offered in language,
dramatic and literary subjects, conversation, schools,
story-telling, singing, dancing, recitation, art, music,
history, gardening, domestic economy, and cottage in-
dustries.

This week the Phibsborough Bazaar is in full swing
at the Rotunda. The Bazaar is being held with a
view of taking something off the heavy debt incurred by
the Vincentian Fathers in enlarging and improving St.
Peter’s Church, Phibsborough.  The history of the
church dates from 1827, at which time it consisted of the
upper storey of a school building. Our readers who
visit the Bazaar will be particularly interested in the
Irish industry stall.  During the night the Rotunda
grounds are illuminated.

Those of our readers who have business relations with
the Royal Trish Industries Association will be interested
to learn that the Association has removed from its old
address in Lincoln Place to a more central site.  The
new premises of the Association are at 76 Grafton St.

THE PRESENT SITUATION.

Various minds in the country are, in the present
situation, naturally moving, and moving quite inde-
pendently in common directions. As for Home Rule
or legislation directly in the way of Home Rule, that
is on the shelf for a good while to come. The Irish
Party had better attempt to retrieve their position some-
what by tackling the University Question in the next
session, for the rest there are many other useful odd
jobs that they may do from time to time. The Par-
liamentary Party, as a consequence of the Council Bill
debacle, have slumped badly, and there is no use blink-
ing the fact.

Instinctively the country turns to the over-taxation
question. We print in another column a letter from a
correspondent on the Financial Relations Problem. He
winds up by asking: ‘‘ How is England to be compelled
to disgorge?’ And he answers: ‘ That is the question
for the politicians.”” So it is, but then we are all poli-
ticians.  When the specialised politicians get their
mandate, it is certainly for them to carry it out skil-
fully, for only a few people in a country can be
specialised politicians. The over-taxation question is
not in a ripe state to be handed over to those who
specialise in politics, it is still a question for the people
at large. We notice that Mr. Andrew Kettle has
issued his promised manifesto. Mr. Kettle’s name, as
Parnell is said to have declared on one occasion, is a
household word in Treland. Mr. Kettle is rather pessi-
mistic. We can well understand the pessimistic humour
of such men as Mr. Kettle and Mr. Webb who have
been in the fight for thirty or forty years, and who
have followed the ‘‘ no-far-distant-date,”” which, as they
followed, fled. There were ‘‘ battles on the floor of the
House *’ thirty years ago, yet we are still without Home
Rule. Certain quack logicians would say that as ‘‘ the
floor of the House’’ was occupied, and as we have not
yet got Home Rule, therefore the Irish Party, or, as
they term it, Parliamentarianism, is the cause of our
failures. A man must be very young or very foolish to
be deceived by this bogus syllogism. Mr. Martyn and
Mr. Sweetman, apparently, are deceived somewhat by
it, and neither of them is very young. It is not a
Party in the House of Commons that thwarts us, but

The Parl'iamentarians loom too largely on 
the Irish horizon, that fact is due largely to the iucom-

ment. 
A moment's thought, however, shows plainly

.E saw last week that the letters used at, presen.t 
for printing and writing English are not of 
English origin. 
We saw f~r~her, that t~e~ are Rom3:n 
letters, devised for the wntmg and prmtrng of Latm 
and Italian. They are accordingly the letters which the 
priest has before him when reading from the Mass book 
at the altar, or when reciting the psalms of his daily 
office. 
Since Rome, however, has been, and is, such a 
great centre of authority and influence, the adoption of 
the Roman alphabet for the printing and writing of 
languages widely differing from Latin and Italian causes 
no surprise, especially when we consider the clearness 
and simplicity of the Roman letters, whether printed or 
written-and in particular for writing purposes if we 
consider the speed with which they can be put down on 
paper. 
When writing words with Irish characters, for 
instance, a person is continually delayed through hav-
ing to lift bis pen in order to dot consonants and stroke 
vowels, while he is again further delayed by having to 
be constantly on the alert lest he should stroke the wrong 
vowel or dot the wrong consonant, o.r dot a consonant 
or stroke a vowel at the wrong time or in the wrong 
place. 
Because most of the consonants are sometimes 
dotted and sometimes not dotted, while all the vowels 
may be stroked or not stroked. 
On the other hand, 
when a person is using the Roman letters he has only 
two letters to dot-namely, " i " and " j," but besides, 
as they are always dotted, one gets into the habit of 
clotting them automatically. Even if they are not dotted 
at all it matters less than in Irish, since the µre~ence 
or absence of the dot does not affect or alter their 
sound. As to strokes over letters, only one letter of 
the Roman alphabet requires a stroke-namely, " t," 
but again the writer draws the stroke through f< t " 
automatically, since "t" is always i-.troked; or even the 
writer may dispense with the stroke by forming " t " in 
another easy and distinctive manner . 
It might be thought that the . using of the Roman or 
Lil.tin letters for other languages would injure the 
accent, sounds, and pronunciation of those languages. 
But that is not the case. 
Letters are arbitrary signs 
which can be made to stand indifferently for any sounds, 
accents, and pronunciations. 
Hence the Roman alpha-
bet lends itself equally well to the spelling and pronun-
ciation of languages which are widely apart i11 sound 
and accent; and speaking absolutely ·.and a prior•i, the 
present Roman letters are at least just as suitable for 
writing the Irish language as the so-called " Irish "

Another and very important reason why the present

hostility to clerical managership; but would it not be as 
easy to say, from the other side, that his advocacy of 
clerical managership is not due to zeal for faith • and

as a right of the office of parish priest, what originated

his propensity for personalities and suspicion he failed

I ~aid I had my children taught at 
home by a tutor, etc.; but th~ whole thing was an 
hypothesis, and the meaning was-" Suppose I, or sup-
pose a person, got his children taught by a tutor, has 
the rector or parish priest the right of appointing the 
tutor, and prescribing the course of secular instruction?" 
but I have not desired to insinuate that I was married 
or that I was single, that I am a priest or that I am 
a layman, that I am a secular or that I am a regular;

themselves, irrespective of who I am or what I am.

tions arises from the fact that he is acquainted only with 
special circumstances where the rule of the Church is not 
realised; that h!;l confounds the regulations made fo1· 
special emergencies with the regulation of the Church

tion to Irish architects of recognised standing.

selves wo.rthy of the country's trust by using for the

ranks with regard to _this question.

account for the amount of material they order from 
abroad 1 ',1.'hey may bring forward the usual plea of

ledge the worth of native materials. If so, tell them,

more nobly with extra thought.

of effect can be got with the minimum of labour in the
